data.title: Wipro: Profit Margin Beat Is Overshadowed By Revenue Guidance Miss
data.description:
I'm staying "Neutral" on Wipro Limited (NYSE:WIT). My view of its above-consensus Q2 margins is favorable. But I'm concerned about the company's worse-than-anticipated growth prospects for the next quarter.

My earlier April 2, 2024, initiation piece outlined my prediction of how WIT will perform across different time horizons.

Profitability Improvement Surpassed Expectations

The firm issued a media release on Thursday, Oct. 16, revealing its 2Q FY2026 (YE end-Mar 2026) financials.

As per S&P Capital IQ data, WIT's recent quarterly bottom line of INR 3.1/share was aligned with what the sell-side had projected. Its meaningful margin expansion was negated by a modestly higher turnover in Jul-Sep this year.

WIT saw its 2Q FY2026 topline rise 1.8% YoY to ₹227.0 billion. This came in below the consensus growth forecast of +2%.

The consumer vertical, accounting for almost one-fifth of company-wide FY2025 revenues, was the weak spot. This end market recorded a 7% year-on-year fall in sales in the latest three-month period adjusted for foreign exchange effects.

At the Q2 media briefing, it was explained that "tariff uncertainties continue to impact the consumer" segment, which impacted these customers' "discretionary spend."

On the flip side, WIT is now more profitable as compared to the same quarter a year ago.

Its normalized EBIT-to-revenue ratio widened by 0.4ppts (percentage points) to 17.2% on YoY terms. That also represented a +60bps (basis points) beat.

WIT indicated at the post-results meeting with journalists that "we have improved our performance operationally." The group's 2Q FY2026 "net (employee) utilization" of 86.4% was 80bps better than what it reported for FY2025. The figures were taken from its results slides.

The company's efforts to enhance efficiency had led to superior margins.

WIT's media call commentary stressed, "our endeavor would be to maintain it (EBIT margin) in this narrow band of an adjusted 17.2%."

The enterprise disclosed at the second quarter analyst meeting that "we are driving several other initiatives still to offset some of these investments that we are making." It specifically mentioned "continuing to optimize" the "Selling, General & Administrative" or "SG&A" expenses.

The SG&A-to-sales for WIT declined from FY2025's 14.6% to 2QFY2026's 13.2%. This metric was below 13% during FY2019-FY2022. I believe there's room for Wipro to become even leaner going forward.

WIT's median EBIT-to-topline was 17.0% for the past decade.

My take is that its commitment to sustaining an operating margin of around "17.2%," or slightly better than historical levels, should provide some form of share price support.

But Management's Growth Outlook Is Disappointing

WIT is guiding for a 0.5% (midpoint) top-line increase for 3Q FY2026 on a QoQ basis. That was weaker than the market's anticipation of +1.6% (source: S&P Capital IQ).

An attendee at the 2Q analyst call, who was from Indian brokerage Emkay, questioned "whether we (Wipro) are witnessing any delay in the deal ramp-up."

I think there is justification for being worried about the pace of converting wins into actual revenues.

The "Total Contract Value" or "TCV" of WIT's bookings grew +32% YoY to $4,688 million. The big jump in this leading indicator appears to be inconsistent with the lackluster forward-looking guide.

An issue might lie with the pipeline composition, in my opinion.

WIT defines "large deal bookings" as "deals greater than or equal to $30 million in total contract value terms" in its earnings presentation. The proportion of "large deals" within its aggregate bookings went up from 42% in 2Q FY2025 to 61% for 2Q FY2026.

WIT commented at its analyst briefing that "we have created a robust engine to go after the large deals" which "are typically cost optimization and/or vendor consolidation." In my view, its focus on this specific category of opportunities could have translated into a decrease in revenue quality.

Mega-projects are generally more complex by virtue of their size, which implies a longer timeline for initial kick-start and eventual completion as well.

McKinsey's research findings show that two-thirds of "large (Information Technology) programs regularly exceed initial budgets, miss schedule estimates, and underdeliver against business objectives."

Wipro acknowledged at the Q2 results meeting with media, "Some of these (large) deals, given the nature, will take more than a few quarters to ramp up."

I fear that WIT's 3Q FY2026 miss might not be a one-off. The firm's concentration in "large deals" poses substantial downside risks to its top-line performance in the near future.

My Closing Remarks

I've decided to leave the existing "Hold" rating for WIT unchanged. The stock is trading at a reasonable valuation.

WIT's current fiscal year P/E is 20 times, which is the same as its historical 10-year average. This is also pretty close to peer Infosys's (INFY) FY 2026 (YE Mar) earnings multiple of 21x. These numbers were sourced from S&P Capital IQ.

The read-throughs from Wipro's 2Q FY2026 disclosures relating to growth and margins are mixed. There are no compelling reasons to be either strongly bullish or bearish on this name.

data.title: BNDX: Understanding Vanguard's Total International Bond ETF
data.description:
In a world where economic growth rates and banking policies differ sharply from one another, international diversification in the bond market can be of interest, where outperforming bond markets can offset losses in underperforming bonds for more consistent and lower volatility long-term returns. The Vanguard Total International Bond ETF (NASDAQ:BNDX) is one of the ETFs that has made international fixed-income diversification easy. Launched on May 31, 2013, the fund has amassed over $109.57 billion in assets under management ("AUM"), with a big reason attributed to its passively-managed index approach that has allowed the fund to offer a low expense ratio of 0.07%. This article will explain how BNDX operates, some of the pros and cons behind investing in the fund, and brief comparisons with other similar funds to help you decide whether BNDX is suitable for you or not.

BNDX's Strategy

Per BNDX's prospectus, the fund seeks to track the performance of a benchmark index that measures the investment return of non-U.S. dollar-denominated investment-grade bonds. In layman's terms, BNDX essentially offers the performance of the overall international bond market excluding the USA's. The index that BNDX tracks is the Bloomberg Global Aggregate ex-USD Float Adjusted RIC Capped Index. This is an index that includes government, government agency, corporate, and securitized non-U.S. investment-grade bonds, all with maturities of greater than one year. The index is also market-cap weighted, but has weighting caps where exposure to any particular bond issuer may not exceed 25% of the fund’s assets and aggregate exposure to issuers that individually constitute 5% or more of the fund may not exceed 50% of the fund’s assets. As of October 16, 2025, BNDX has the most exposure to France, Japan, and Germany, and exposure to 42 different countries. Holdings are rebalanced monthly.

It should also be noted that BNDX employs a sampling method to replicate index returns and hedges returns to USD. Instead of buying and holding the tens of thousands of bonds in the index, a sampling method allows BNDX to track the index by selecting a group of bonds that collectively generate the same returns. This allows BNDX to manage the fund more efficiently and avoid problems generated from buying potentially illiquid bonds, though there may be tracking error involved, as you will note below. BNDX's holdings are still in the thousands, with 6581 holdings as of August 31, 2025.

Another characteristic of BNDX is that it will use swaps and currency contracts to hedge returns to USD. This way, what BNDX provides is more or less the performance of the bonds themselves and not necessarily how the USD is performing relative to the foreign currencies.

Speaking of swaps and currency contracts, any positive returns you get due to an USD-hedge will generally come out as ordinary income and that extends to entire fund. Interest earnings are typically taxed at ordinary income rates and many countries often don't have withholding taxes on interest payments for non-residents. Thus, the result is that very little foreign tax credit can be claimed. Per Vanguard's 2024 foreign tax credit information, BNDX only paid 0.20% in foreign taxes. Part of this can be reclaimed with foreign tax credits but the downside is that you may lose benefits from state and local tax laws that exempt taxes on U.S.-domiciled bonds. BNDX will also distribute any capital gains at the end of the year which come from the fund's potential gains during rebalancing. Thus, please consult a professional tax advisor to see if the fund is suitable for you.

BNDX Use Cases And Investor Suitability

Given BNDX's diverse holdings and position as an international aggregate bond fund that excludes U.S. bonds, the primary use case for BNDX is to diversify an investor's bond holding s, particularly if you're heavily concentrated in U.S. bonds. In a backtest done on Portfolio Visualizer spanning over the last ten years, a 50/50 allocation of the Vanguard Total Bond Market ETF (BND) and BNDX would have outperformed a 100% allocation to BND with lower drawdowns and less volatility. The compound annual growth rate ("CAGR") of the former would be at 1.96% compared to the latter's 1.80%. Maximum drawdowns were at 15.81% versus 17.54% and standard deviation levels were also better at 4.58% versus 5.16%.

While low costs and a passive index approach can make BNDX suitable for long-term buy-and-hold investors, an index approach also makes BNDX suitable for more active investors and traders. Bonds are generally mean-reverting, where their values will typically return to historical averages over the long run as opposed to an asset class like equities, which are expected to rise over time.

This is because interest rates will neither continuously increase or decrease indefinitely. Rates are generally cut to increase the money supply for various reasons like stimulating the economy. Rate cuts, however, have an inflationary effect and central banks tend to counter effects of inflation by hiking interest rates and decreasing the money supply. But as rate hikes make it more expensive to borrow, economic activity tends to slow and eventually require central banks to cut rates again. Thus, a common strategy among investors is to reduce bond allocations when interest rates fall, while increasing them when interest rates rise.

As previously mentioned in the introduction, the economies and monetary policies of countries globally can differ from each other significantly. For example, the following chart shows the latest interest rates of various central banks around the world and what their previous rates were. It also shows when the change was made and a key observation is how these changes can often differ greatly from when the Fed announces their interest rate changes.

Hence, more active investors and traders can look to capture the returns from the discrepancy in rate cutting and hiking cycles from different countries and dynamically adjust their bond allocations in funds like BNDX and BND according to U.S. and international bond markets.

BNDX Key Risks And Considerations

Of course, if one were going to invest in international assets for the diversification and potentially better return benefits, one must also consider key risks with investing internationally. Researchers and analysts often discuss investing in the context of the USA versus the rest of the world, but it is important to remember that the "rest of the world" includes many countries that individually differ from each other significantly. For example, Japan's monetary policy has differed significantly from Canada's where it has kept near-zero interest rates for about a decade until it started hiking interest rates at the end of 2024 and beginning of 2025. Canada during the same 2024-2025 period has been cutting interest rates to stimulate economic activity. Moreover, BNDX is not a fund that only holds bonds from developed markets. Plenty of emerging market bonds have been included as well. BNDX, for example, holds bonds from countries that have historically defaulted on their debt before like Peru and Greece. And this isn't to say that these bonds are likely to default again, but bonds from emerging markets can require a different kind of analysis compared to ones from developed markets.

With these international investing risks in mind, it should be noted that passive management can also be a risk in itself. This is especially the case with bond funds where market cap weightings that many indices use are suboptimal when considering bonds across multiple issuers and durations. Unlike equities where high market caps are typically a result of the underlying company doing well, high market caps in one bond issuer means that the issuer is loaning a lot of money and that has no indication on the quality of the issuer. It could mean that the issuer is doing well and wants financing to grow fast or it could mean the issuer is in trouble and is in need of financing to survive. A passive index approach does not assess for these qualities and is the reason why active fund managers are often able to outperform benchmarks in the bond industry. According to PIMCO, more than 75% of global aggregate and global investment grade corporate managers have outperformed their passive counterparts between 2014 and 2023. Thus, passive long-term investors may potentially be better off investing in actively-managed bond funds instead.

Comparison With Other Funds

One actively-managed fund worth comparing against is the PIMCO Multisector Bond Active Exchange-Traded Fund (PYLD). This is an actively-managed bond ETF that trades across multiple different bond sectors including both USA bonds and international. And while PYLD has only a little more than two years of history at the time of writing, you can already see how active management has helped the fund generate stronger returns than BNDX despite harboring a 0.55% expense ratio.

Of course, one cannot compare bond ETFs with BNDX without discussing the iShares Core International Aggregate Bond ETF (IAGG). This fund has $11.29B in AUM and has similar goals of providing convenient access to the international bond market excluding USA bonds. IAGG also only charges a 0.07% management fee and since its inception, has tracked closely to BNDX until 2022. But one huge differentiating factor is that IAGG pays distributions yearly while BNDX pays monthly. If payout frequency is of concern, then BNDX is generally preferable. Otherwise, comparing the two funds' underlying strategic differences is required.

Performance differences between the two funds can be attributed to their respective index methodologies. BNDX tracks the Bloomberg Global Aggregate ex-USD Float Adjusted RIC Capped Index while IAGG tracks the Bloomberg Global Aggregate ex-USD 10% Issuer Capped Index; both hedge their returns to USD. The difference between IAGG and BNDX stems from how each respective index determines market weights and caps. One of the primary differences you'll notice when studying the two funds is that IAGG has a significantly higher exposure to China than BNDX at over 18% compared to approximately 1%.

The main reason for this, as well as the other discrepancies in holdings, is that the float-adjusted market weighting in BNDX makes it so that the fund will invest in assets that are freely available to a typical investor versus a normal market weighting that could include assets that are not necessarily available to your average retail investor. What that means is that BNDX will generally have a greater concentration of assets in developed markets while IAGG will often have more emerging market exposure. The following chart supports this as an emerging market bond ETF in VWOB has outperformed a developed markets bond ETF in IGOV in the same time that IAGG has outperformed BNDX.

Your choice between IAGG and BNDX will ultimately come down to a preference for more emerging market bonds or developed market bonds if dividend payout frequency is of no concern.

BNDX Conclusion

With a low expense ratio and a passively-managed index approach, BNDX can be a valuable ETF for passive and active investors alike who want exposure to the international bond market. But as with all foreign investments, investors must consider the risks that come with exposure to foreign markets. BNDX investors also need to understand the potential risks with passively-managed bond funds and the flaw with a market cap weighting approach. It is always worthwhile to consider what options are available like actively-managed bond funds or direct BNDX competitors like IAGG.

This article answers these three main questions about BNDX:

  1. What are BNDX's holdings and how is it constructed?
  2. How can BNDX fit in a portfolio?
  3. What tax implications and risks pertain to BNDX?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: Bank Profits Accelerate
data.description:
In the third quarter of 2025, banking profits accelerated.

Take a look at this...

In the third quarter of 2025:

JPMorgan Chase reported a profit of $14.4 billion...up 12 percent over the third quarter of 2024;

Goldman Sachs reported a profit of $4.1 billion...up 37 percent over the third quarter of 2024;

Citigroup reported a profit of $3.8 billion...up 16 percent over the third quarter of 2024;

and,

Wells Fargo reported a profit of $5.6 billion...up 9 percent over the third quarter of 2024.

Wow!!!

The word reported by Stacy Cowley to describe the economy that produced this performance was "resilient".

The U.S. economy bounced in the third quarter of 2025.

Jamie Dimon, CEO of JPMorgan Chase, describes the situation: "While there have been some signs of a softening, particularly in job growth, the U.S. economy generally remained resilient."

And, as I have been constantly writing, the U.S. banking system has been extremely liquid, therefore having on hand the resources to underwrite the expansion.

The exceptional performance showed up in many places...not necessarily, however, in the basic business of commercial banking.

The market conditions dictated where the exceptional performance came from.

"Traders" took advantage of the volatility experienced by the markets.

"Revenue from trading and its markets businesses rose 25%."

Mergers and taking companies public flourished, and investment banking fees jumped 16 percent in the third quarter of 2025 from the third quarter of 2024.

"The bank has helped underwrite a number of the year's biggest deals."

Consumer banking also did well as consumers continued to spend...debit and credit card spending rose significantly.

And earnings per share came in at $5.07 for the quarter, above an expected $4.85 per share.

Overall...well done.

The performance of JPMorgan Chase provided a picture of third-quarter results that was copied by many other of the "BIG" banks.

Each of these large banks did well and in a similar fashion.

The strength of the performances was, in a sense, duplicated by the others, but in most cases led to their own particular shining results.

For example, Wells Fargo perceived that their situation had changed sufficiently that they could change one of their target goals.

Going forward, it was reported that Wells Fargo was going to raise its target for its return on tangible common equity to between 17% and 18%.

The previous target: 15%.

I believe that this is a very, very important move.

In the past, 15% used to be the rate that the large major commercial banks set for their target returns because it was estimated that this rate was about as good as you could get.

JPMorgan Chase had a higher target rate than this, a higher rate than the other major large commercial banks, but it was considered to be the exception.

JPMorgan Chase did produce returns that were around the level of 18% or so, but JPMorganChase was considered to be the exception.

JPMorgan Chase was at the top of the field. And it proved it, year after year.

Now, here is Wells Fargo saying that it is going after a return that would be in the range of 17% or 18%...not just an ordinary 15%.

I truly believe that this is saying something important about where the commercial banking system is at the present time.

Commercial banks are in a strong position.

Commercial banks are performing...not necessarily in the areas "commercial banks" usually star in, but are now finding opportunities to use the excess amounts of liquidity they have on hand to produce higher returns.

They are responding to the market conditions that have been created by the new way to "Make America Great Again."

These banks are doing what they should be doing, given the conditions.

And, as one looks through the results posted by the other major banks that have reported, we are seeing the same things.

Take a look at the headlines reporting the third-quarter results of Citigroup: "Citigroup's Profit Jumps, Boosted by Dealmaking and Trading."

"A steady stream of sizable mergers in recent months has padded revenues for Wall Street banks...."

"Revenue from Citigroup's banking unit was up 34% from the year-earlier period...."

"Trading revenue rose 15%...."

"Net interest income...rose 12%."

And then there was the report from Goldman Sachs.

The headlines: "Goldman Sachs Profit Surges, Fueled by Dealmaking Boom."

Goldman Sachs is on its way to its best year ever in its main investment banking and markets division.

Imagine.

The Federal Reserve System has been following a policy of "quantitative tightening," and it has been following this policy for more than three-and-a-half years.

Can you imagine?

Goldman Sachs is experiencing a "Dealmaking Boom"!

And that is where we are in the banking industry.

The banking industry is in good shape.

The banking industry is doing well...very well.

The American economy moves on.

The American economy has all sorts of money lying around in bank vaults.

We are now really starting to feel the impact of the Fed's efforts in the early 2020s to prevent a major economic catastrophe. The Fed injected several trillions of dollars into the banking system to make sure it did not fold.

The banking system did not fold.

The Federal Reserve tried to remove as much of the excess as it could.

It has been reducing its securities portfolio for a long time.

The question now appears to be...the Fed has not done enough.

Oh, boy..........

data.title: TrustCo Bank: I'm A Buyer At These Levels
data.description:
Introduction

TrustCo Bank Corp (NASDAQ:TRST) is the holding company of the Trustco Bank, which has almost 140 offices with a specific focus on the New York and Florida markets. I have always liked the bank’s very robust loan book, which allows it to keep the loan loss provisions low. Moreover, the revenue in its financial services and wealth management is increasing as well, and while this is just a minor contributor to the general result, I like the additional diversification this could offer further down the road and rate shares a "Buy."

A look back at the first semester before the Q3 results come out

In the second quarter of this year, the bank reported a total interest income of almost $64.5M, which was a $4 million increase compared to the interest income it generated in the second quarter of last year. Meanwhile, the interest expenses remained stable (there was a decrease of less than 1%), and this ultimately resulted in a net interest income of $41.7 million, which is an increase of approximately 10% compared to the second quarter of last year.

Meanwhile, the bank’s non-interest income decreased slightly, mainly because it didn’t repeat the gain on the sale of equity securities from Q2 2025. That being said, the non-interest expenses also decreased slightly, and this resulted in a pre-tax and pre-provision income of $20.4 million for the quarter. A robust improvement over the $17 million in Q2 2024.

One of the key elements to like TrustCo is its consistently low amount of loan loss provisions. I will discuss the quality of the loan book in the next section of this article, but it goes without saying that keeping the provisions for loan losses limited is a key element to protect the profitability of the bank. The bottom line of TrustCo shows a net profit of just over $15 million for an EPS of $0.79. The EPS for the entire first semester came in at $1.54, which is almost 20% higher than the net income per share for the first half of last year.

The bank currently pays a (recently raised) quarterly dividend of $0.38 per share, and it goes without saying that these dividends are very well covered, as the payout ratio is less than 50%.

The loan book: Manageable amount of loans past due

As mentioned before, keeping the loan loss provisions at a low level is a key element to protect the bottom line. TrustCo has an excellent history of keeping those provisions pretty low. As you can see below, the total size of the loan book is just over $5.1 billion (after taking the accumulated allowance into account), while the bank runs a pretty liquid balance sheet with in excess of $700 million in cash and an additional $340 million in securities.

Looking at the breakdown of the loan book, the bank clearly doesn’t have a high exposure to commercial real estate, which makes up less than 6% of the loan book. It does have a very high exposure to residential real estate, mainly in New York and Florida.

While those may not be every investor’s favorite states to have exposure to, it’s also important to take a look at the total amount of loans past due. And as you can see below, the vast majority of the loan book is current. At the end of the second quarter, only $17 million of the loan book was classified as past due, and pretty much all of those soured loans are backed by real estate.

Unfortunately, the bank has not provided any background on the average LTV ratios in the real estate portfolio, but given its history of dealing pretty swiftly with non-performing loans, I’m not anticipating any near-term issues. This also means the bank should be able to keep its loan loss provisions relatively limited on a quarterly basis, as the provisions currently represent almost 300% of the loans past due. And considering the vast majority of those loans past due are secured by real estate, I doubt the bank risks taking a big haircut on the $17 million in loans past due.

Investment thesis

TrustCo Bank runs a very robust balance sheet, and the quality of the loan book appears to be excellent. The low loan loss provisions are a key element to the bank’s profitability levels, and together with the relatively low dividend payout ratio of less than 50%, TrustCo can increase its book value and tangible book value at a pretty decent pace.

At the end of the second quarter, the TBVPS was approximately $36.72 per share, which means the stock is currently trading at a small discount to its tangible book value per share. That makes the stock very attractive at the current levels, not in the least because you’re also picking up stock at an earnings multiple of just 10 times the net profit. Meanwhile, the TBVPS should increase to approximately $40/share by the end of next year, and that adds to the appeal.

I currently have no position in TrustCo Bank, but I will likely initiate a long position in the near future.

data.title: Jefferies Stock Drop On Credit Cockroaches Looks Like Fear Selling, Still A Buy
data.description:
Jefferies (NYSE:JEF) shares have come under pressure in recent weeks as a pair of consumer-related bankruptcies have sparked new concerns about the state of the lending market. It has the feel of March 2023 all over again, and we saw U.S. regional bank stocks crater on Thursday, Oct. 16.

I have a more sanguine view, though. First, recency bias is a powerful force in investors' minds right now, and a “shoot first, ask questions later” environment seems to be the M.O. Second, the U.S. government shutdown is likely putting an undue spotlight on what could end up being minor macro issues. Finally, the consumer remains in decent shape, as evidenced by the percentage of underwater car loans that remains below pre-pandemic levels.

I have a "Buy" rating on JEF, seen as an at-risk lender in the private credit space. I had a "Buy" rating on the stock back in January 2023, and shares are up 63% in that span. That’s close to the S&P 500’s return, and the current pullback is an opportunity, in my view.

Underwater Car Loans Still Below 2019-2020 Levels

JEF is an off-season reporter, and back in September, the firm recorded a solid set of Q3 results. Q3 GAAP EPS of $1.01 topped the Wall Street consensus forecast of $0.69, while revenue of $2.05 billion, up a strong 22% from the same period last year, was a material $125 million beat. Its return on adjusted tangible shareholders' equity from continuing operations was 13.6%.

Shares dipped following the robust quarterly update, however. The 1.9% slide came amid a downturn more broadly in the industry. Today, JEF’s implied volatility level is high at 73%, resulting in a 10% options straddle priced in ahead of its next earnings report, not due out until Jan. 7.

Looking back on the quarter that was, the top and bottom line beats were driven by a rebound in investment banking fees and healthy trading revenue. Its Advisory segment net sales rose 10.7% year over year to $656 million, aided by lower trade-policy uncertainty and revived M&A activity.

In terms of overall banking performance, combined advisory and underwriting revenue climbed 17%. Higher deal flow and improved overall sentiment in the sector were clear tailwinds, though the vibes have certainly shifted since September.

Then, just earlier this week, JEF hosted its annual Investor Day. At the event, the management team outlined a long-term plan, but traders were much more focused on its First Brands exposure than on the overall strategy. Analysts see about $161 million of net financial exposure, which is somewhat small compared to JEF’s $10.1 billion market cap. Before the Investor Day, the company’s executives said it could withstand First Brand losses.

On the earnings outlook, EPS growth is seen as about flat for FY 2025, while out-year per-share earnings estimates are much stronger. EPS growth is then seen rising 28% by FY 2027. With strong operating leverage predicted, the sell-side has actually been upbeat on the stock—there have been four earnings upgrades over the past 90 days compared to zero downgrades.

Jefferies: Revenue & Earnings Forecasts, EPS Revision Trends

On valuation, I did not provide an intrinsic value target in my previous assessment. Today, if we assume $3.99 or normalized EPS and apply its 5-year average 15x price-to-earnings multiple, then shares should trade near $60.

That makes today’s $49 price quite attractive. What’s more, JEF’s operating EPS could reach $5 in 2027. Shares also trade under 1.0x time book.

JEF: Now Cheap on a P/E and P/B Basis

Key risks for JEF include a slower M&A environment if we see a macro downturn. Furthermore, if there are more cockroaches in the system, the company's private credit and private equity exposure could be at risk.

JEF has relatively low exposure to bulge-bracket banks and less revenue diversification, so there are some idiosyncratic financial risks. Its trading activity is less risky compared to years ago, while the balance sheet’s leverage is in check.

Competitor Analysis

Looking ahead, corporate event data provided by Wall Street Horizon show an unconfirmed Q4 2025 earnings date of Wednesday, Jan. 7 AMC. Before that, shares traded ex a $0.40 dividend on Monday, Nov. 17.

The management team is also slated to speak at the SmartCon Conference 2025 early next month.

Corporate Event Calendar

The Technical Take

With shares down sharply and now a solid value in my view, JEF’s technical situation is troubled. Notice in the chart below that the stock has been nearly cut in half from its early-year high. It’s also down by more than 30% from the peak just less than a month ago. Today, the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph is firmly in technical oversold territory, suggesting that we could see a bounce.

But take a look at the long-term 200-day moving average. It’s negatively sloped, suggesting that the bears control the primary trend. I see resistance in the mid-$60s, while support is down at $39. The recent volume spike associated with the stock drop is worrisome, but given the recency bias in play within the regional banking space (though JEF is certainly not a regional bank), I assert this is more a fear-driven selloff than a reality-based selloff.

JEF: $39 Key Support, Oversold RSI, Volume Concerns, Falling 200dma

The Bottom Line

I have a "Buy" rating on JEF. I see the stock as unduly beaten down by what’s so far a somewhat small and manageable financial hit related to First Brands. Its Investor Day did not reveal any additional issues, and shares are undervalued versus history now.
